stories.title: Cursor Automations in Agents Window
stories.summary:
↳ WHAT'S NEW
Cursor Automations moved into the Agents Window — previously only accessible at cursor.com/automations, they now live alongside agents in the same interface. Two new automation types ship with this: multi-repo automations (agents reasoning across multiple codebases simultaneously) and no-repo automations (agents that monitor external tools like Slack or data warehouses and act on signals without any attached repository).

↳ THE REAL STORY
The consolidation is mostly a UX win — one less context switch between managing automations and managing agents. The more substantive change is the no-repo automation type. It lets you wire an agent to a non-code signal source — a Slack message, a warehouse query result, a metric threshold — and have it act without needing a codebase as the entry point. That's a meaningful expansion of where Cursor agents can sit in a workflow: not just "triggered by a PR" but "triggered by anything with an API." Multi-repo support matters for monorepo-adjacent setups where a change in a shared library needs to be reasoned about in the context of downstream consumers.
stories.considerations:
↳ WORTH WATCHING
The no-repo automation path is the one to stress-test carefully. Agents acting on external signals without a code anchor — monitoring Slack, querying warehouses — is where scope creep and unintended side effects get hard to audit. Cursor hasn't published much on the permission model or action boundaries for these automations. Before wiring one to anything production-adjacent, the open question is: what does the blast radius look like if the agent misreads a signal, and is there a meaningful approval gate before it acts?

stories.title: Amazon Q Developer agentic capabilities on SWE-Bench
stories.summary:
↳ WHAT'S NEW
Amazon Q Developer claimed the top spot on both SWE-Bench and SWE-Bench Lite leaderboards — the standard benchmarks for evaluating AI agents on real GitHub issues across open-source Python repos.

↳ THE REAL STORY
SWE-Bench measures whether an agent can actually resolve filed GitHub issues end-to-end: read the repo, locate the bug, write a fix, pass the tests. Topping both the full and Lite variants suggests Q Developer's agentic loop — planning, file editing, test execution — is genuinely competitive on multi-step tasks, not just autocomplete. For teams already on AWS, this is a concrete reason to revisit Q Developer for PR review automation and test generation rather than treating it as a second-tier Copilot alternative.
stories.considerations:
↳ WORTH WATCHING
SWE-Bench scores are notoriously sensitive to evaluation methodology: which scaffold, how many retries, whether the agent sees the test suite. Amazon hasn't published a technical report detailing their setup, which makes the number hard to verify or reproduce. The leaderboard also shifts fast — a top score today can be third place in six weeks. Before routing real engineering workflows through Q Developer based on this claim, it's worth waiting for an independent replication or at least a detailed methodology disclosure from Amazon.

stories.title: Claude Opus 4.7 fast mode now available
stories.summary:
↳ WHAT'S NEW
Windsurf added a fast mode for Claude Opus 4.7, claiming roughly 2.5x higher output speeds versus standard mode with no change to the underlying model.

↳ THE REAL STORY
For developers using Opus 4.7 in Windsurf, this is a latency cut that directly affects tight edit-run-debug loops. If the 2.5x figure holds under real workloads, multi-file refactors and long completions that previously felt sluggish become practical for interactive use rather than something you kick off and context-switch away from. The capability ceiling stays the same — you're getting the same model, just served faster.
stories.considerations:
↳ WORTH WATCHING
The obvious question is how Windsurf is achieving this — speculative decoding, a different inference backend, or simply routing to a faster hardware tier. That distinction matters because 'fast mode' at 2.5x often comes with a cost trade-off buried somewhere: higher token pricing, reduced context window, or throughput throttling under load. Windsurf hasn't published the mechanism or the pricing delta, so treat the speed claim as a best-case number until independent benchmarks surface.

stories.title: Secure MCP Tunnel for enterprise customers
stories.summary:
↳ WHAT'S NEW
OpenAI's Secure MCP Tunnel lets enterprise customers connect private or on-premises MCP servers to ChatGPT web, Codex, Responses API, and AgentKit — via a customer-hosted tunnel client, no direct internet exposure required.

↳ THE REAL STORY
The blocker for most enterprise MCP adoption has been straightforward: internal tools, databases, and APIs sit behind firewalls that OpenAI's infrastructure can't reach. This tunnel client flips the connection direction — your infrastructure initiates outbound, OpenAI's products consume it. In practice, that means a Codex agent can now call an internal Jira instance, a private code registry, or an on-prem data warehouse without punching holes in the firewall or routing sensitive data through a public endpoint. The integration surface for agentic workflows just got significantly wider for regulated industries.
stories.considerations:
↳ WORTH WATCHING
The tunnel client is customer-hosted — which means your team owns the operational burden: deployment, uptime, patching, and monitoring. If the tunnel goes down, every connected OpenAI product loses access to those MCP servers silently or noisily depending on how well you've instrumented it. There's also a trust question worth sitting with: traffic still transits OpenAI's infrastructure on the other end of that tunnel. For teams in heavily regulated environments (finance, healthcare, government), 'no direct internet exposure' is not the same as 'data never leaves our perimeter' — and that distinction will matter when the compliance team asks.

stories.title: Filter session history by workspace directory
stories.summary:
↳ WHAT'S NEW
Continue.dev added workspace-scoped filtering to session history — chat logs can now be viewed per workspace directory instead of as one global list.

↳ THE REAL STORY
If you run Continue across several repos simultaneously, the session list was previously a flat pile of conversations with no project context. Now you can open a workspace and see only the chats that happened there. Practically: faster retrieval of that debugging thread from last Tuesday without scrolling past unrelated sessions from three other codebases. Small UX change, real friction removed for anyone juggling more than two active projects.
stories.considerations: The feature is only as useful as your workspace hygiene. If you habitually open files ad-hoc outside a defined workspace root, or work in monorepos where multiple logical projects share one folder, the filter won't give you the granularity you actually want. Worth checking whether Continue scopes by the exact root path or does something smarter — because a monorepo with five services all under one directory will still show you everything.
